name: California Dept of General Services (DGS)
tagline: California's central business-services agency and the gateway for how state entities buy: DGS runs statewide procurement (leveraged procurement agreements, CMAS, statewide contracts), grants delegated purchasing authority, and manages the state's fleet and real estate.
overview: The California Department of General Services (DGS) is the state's central business-services agency — it runs statewide procurement, grants delegated purchasing authority to departments, and manages the state fleet, real estate, and building services. For a technology seller, DGS is best understood as the front door to how California state (and many local) entities buy goods and IT. Its Procurement Division (PD) holds statutory authority to acquire non-IT and IT goods and services for the state and to award Leveraged Procurement Agreements (LPAs) — the umbrella that includes California Multiple Award Schedules (CMAS), Master Service Agreements, statewide contracts, and cooperative agreements that state and local agencies can buy off without running their own solicitation. DGS also accredits departments to make their own delegated purchases. Note the division of labor with the California Department of Technology (CDT): DGS holds the underlying purchasing authority, but CDT has statutory authority over reportable/non-delegated IT projects and runs statewide technology procurement oversight — so larger IT deals typically touch both. Most supplier interaction flows through Cal eProcure, the state's online procurement portal and contracts register.

budget.intro: DGS is funded largely through cost recovery on the services it provides (the Service Revolving Fund and related funds) rather than a large general-fund appropriation — its leverage is the volume of procurement that flows through its vehicles, not its own spend. The Governor's proposed 2026-27 budget includes targeted technology-services investments routed through DGS and partner funds.

stats.label: Primary funding model
stats.note: DGS recovers costs from the agencies it serves rather than a large GF line

stats.label: Digital ID as a service
stats.note: Proposed 2026-27 Technology Services Revolving Fund expansion (statewide digital services)

stats.label: Digital services scale-up
stats.note: Proposed 2026-27 reimbursement authority for design, data, and digital service delivery

notes: DGS is the path, not the wallet — the largest end-IT budgets sit with departments like CDT, HHS agencies, and others that buy through DGS vehicles.
Exact DGS-line figures shift between the January proposed budget and the May Revision; confirm against ebudget.ca.gov before forecasting.

initiatives.name: Cal eProcure & procurement modernization
initiatives.summary: DGS continues to operate and refine Cal eProcure as the state's procurement portal and contracts register. Supplier registration, LPA/CMAS records, and the California State Contracts Register all run through it — the system to master for visibility and access.

initiatives.name: Statewide digital services & Digital ID
initiatives.summary: The 2026-27 budget proposes expanding statewide digital service delivery and Digital ID as a service through DGS-related technology revolving funds — a track that pulls in design, data, and identity infrastructure.

initiatives.name: Fleet electrification (ZEV)
initiatives.summary: DGS manages the state fleet and issues the statewide vehicle purchasing contract; California's ZEV mandate requires 50% of state/local fleet purchases be ZEV/NZEV through 2026, moving to 100% from 2027 — infrastructure and charging follow the fleet.

initiatives.name: Delegated purchasing authority program
initiatives.summary: Through the Statewide Procurement Oversight and Consulting unit (SPOC, formerly PAU), DGS accredits departments to make their own purchases within set thresholds — shaping which deals are run centrally versus at the department level.

procurement.intro: Selling to California state entities runs through DGS-awarded vehicles and the Cal eProcure portal, not one-off RFPs. Two things to confirm first: that Dell holds the right active LPA/CMAS coverage and correct scope, and — for larger IT — how CDT's IT-project authority overlays the buy.

vehicles.name: Leveraged Procurement Agreements (LPAs)
vehicles.detail: DGS's umbrella of pre-competed agreements (statutory authority under PCC 10290.1 / 10298). Includes CMAS, Master Service Agreements, statewide contracts, and cooperative agreements that state and local agencies can buy off directly.

vehicles.name: California Multiple Award Schedules (CMAS)
vehicles.detail: Statewide schedule contracts, frequently based on federal GSA schedules, covering IT and non-IT products and services at pre-assessed fair/reasonable pricing. A primary path for commodity and solution sales to state and local buyers.

vehicles.name: Statewide contracts
vehicles.detail: DGS-awarded mandatory or optional-use statewide agreements (e.g., commodity, fleet) that consolidate the state's buying power. Confirm whether a category is mandatory-use before pursuing alternatives.

vehicles.name: Cal eProcure
vehicles.detail: The state's procurement portal — supplier registration, LPA/CMAS records, and the California State Contracts Register (bid opportunities). The system to register in and monitor for access and visibility.

powerStructure.name: Ana M. Lasso
powerStructure.title: Director, DGS
powerStructure.note: Appointed by Gov. Newsom in March 2021; leads the central business-services agency. The top relationship for the DGS account. Confirmed on the DGS executive-staff page as of June 2026.

powerStructure.name: Elizabeth Williamson
powerStructure.title: Chief Deputy Director, DGS
powerStructure.note: Appointed October 2025. DGS lists two Chief Deputy Directors (Williamson and Jason Kenney) as of June 2026 — second-in-command tier across DGS operations.

powerStructure.name: Jason Kenney
powerStructure.title: Chief Deputy Director, DGS
powerStructure.note: Long-tenured DGS executive (prior Deputy Director, Real Estate Services); appears as a Chief Deputy Director on the current executive-staff page alongside Williamson.

powerStructure.name: Angela Shell
powerStructure.title: Deputy Director, Procurement Division & Chief Procurement Officer
powerStructure.note: Has led PD/CPO since 2017 (reappointed 2021), still current as of June 2026 — owns LPAs, CMAS, statewide contracts, and delegated authority. The core procurement relationship.

powerStructure.name: Gary Renslo
powerStructure.title: Chief Information Officer, DGS (Enterprise Technology Solutions)
powerStructure.note: DGS's own CIO — oversees the department's applications, infrastructure, and enterprise architecture (distinct from statewide CDT). Relevant for DGS's internal IT and Cal eProcure-adjacent modernization.

powerStructure.name: Statewide Procurement Oversight & Consulting (SPOC)
powerStructure.title: Delegated purchasing authority unit
powerStructure.note: Formerly the Purchasing Authority Unit; accredits departments to buy on their own — shapes central vs. department-level deal flow.

powerStructure.name: California Dept of Technology (CDT)
powerStructure.title: Statewide IT procurement & project authority
powerStructure.note: Separate agency, but holds authority over reportable/non-delegated IT projects — larger IT deals typically touch both DGS and CDT.

news.title: Executive Order N-5-26 directs DGS and CDT to develop AI vendor certifications for state contracting (120-day deliverable, ~late July 2026)
news.impact: Highly relevant to any AI-capable technology vendor: DGS + CDT must recommend new vendor certifications (attesting to safeguards against illegal content, harmful model bias, and civil-rights/civil-liberties impacts) that may be folded into state contracting. Watch for the recommendations landing ~late July 2026 and how they attach to LPAs/CMAS and new solicitations; align Dell AI-related products/attestations early. Recommendations are subject to gubernatorial approval before adoption.

news.title: Cal eProcure modernization pushed as part of Newsom's procurement-reform agenda (EO N-30-25)
news.impact: Ongoing pressure to redesign Cal eProcure into a more e-commerce-like platform (jointly with CDT) surfaces DGS as the owner of the state's supplier-facing procurement front door. A next-gen portal build/procurement would itself be an opportunity and would reshape supplier registration, bid visibility, and LPA/CMAS search. Track for scope and any solicitation.

news.title: DGS Procurement Division continues SB/DVBE onboarding outreach (e.g., May 13, 2026 'Next Steps to Contracting with California' webinar)
news.impact: Reinforces the 25% Small Business / 3% DVBE participation goals departments must hit — relevant to partner/reseller and teaming strategy on California deals routed through DGS vehicles.

news.title: Governor's proposed 2026-27 budget funds statewide digital services and Digital ID as a service through DGS-related revolving funds
news.impact: Signals continued investment in identity and digital-service infrastructure routed through DGS — a track to align solutions to. Confirm final figures in the enacted budget.

news.title: Elizabeth Williamson appointed Chief Deputy Director of DGS
news.impact: New second-in-command across DGS operations — refresh the relationship map at the top of the agency.

dellActions: Confirm Dell's active CMAS and other LPA coverage and scope through Cal eProcure — it's the access path to California state and many local buyers, not just DGS itself.
Register and monitor Cal eProcure and the California State Contracts Register for opportunities, and verify supplier records are current.
Map the CDT overlay for any larger IT deal — DGS holds purchasing authority but CDT governs reportable/non-delegated IT projects; align early with both.
Build the Procurement Division relationship (CPO Angela Shell's org) for vehicle scope, statewide contracts, and delegated-authority dynamics.
Track the 2026-27 digital-services and Digital ID investments and the ZEV fleet transition for infrastructure, identity, and lifecycle pull-through.
